name: adafruit-circuitpython-typing

metadata.name: adafruit-circuitpython-typing
metadata.summary: Types needed for type annotation that are not in `typing`

documentation:
# Introduction

```{image} https://readthedocs.org/projects/adafruit-circuitpython-typing/badge/?version=latest
:alt: Documentation Status
:target: https://docs.circuitpython.org/projects/adafruit-circuitpython-typing/en/latest/
```

```{image} https://github.com/adafruit/Adafruit_CircuitPython_Bundle/blob/main/badges/adafruit_discord.svg
:alt: Discord
:target: https://adafru.it/discord
```

```{image} https://github.com/adafruit/Adafruit_CircuitPython_Typing/workflows/Build%20CI/badge.svg
:alt: Build Status
:target: https://github.com/adafruit/Adafruit_CircuitPython_Typing/actions
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg
:alt: 'Code Style: Black'
:target: https://github.com/psf/black
```

Definitions not in the standard `typing` module that are
needed for type annotation of CircuitPython code.

This library is not needed at runtime for CircuitPython code, and does not need to be in the bundle.

# Dependencies

# Installing from PyPI

On supported GNU/Linux systems like the Raspberry Pi, you can install the driver locally [from
PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/adafruit-circuitpython-typing/).
To install for current user:

```shell
pip3 install adafruit-circuitpython-typing
```

To install system-wide (this may be required in some cases):

```shell
sudo pip3 install adafruit-circuitpython-typing
```

To install in a virtual environment in your current project:

```shell
mkdir project-name && cd project-name
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip3 install adafruit-circuitpython-typing
```

# Documentation

API documentation for this library can be found on [Read the Docs](https://docs.circuitpython.org/projects/adafruit-circuitpython-typing/en/latest/).

For information on building library documentation, please check out
[this guide](https://learn.adafruit.com/creating-and-sharing-a-circuitpython-library/sharing-our-docs-on-readthedocs#sphinx-5-1).

# Contributing

Contributions are welcome! Please read our [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/adafruit/Adafruit_CircuitPython_Typing/blob/HEAD/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md)
before contributing to help this project stay welcoming.

modules.name: circuitpython_typing
documentation:
`circuitpython_typing`
================================================================================

Types needed for type annotation that are not in `typing`


* Author(s): Alec Delaney, Dan Halbert, Randy Hudson
SPDX-FileCopyrightText: Copyright (c) 2022 Dan Halbert for Adafruit Industries
SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT

variables.name: circuitpython_typing.ReadableBuffer
documentation: Classes that implement the readable buffer protocol.

variables.name: circuitpython_typing.WriteableBuffer
documentation: Classes that implement the writeable buffer protocol.

variables.name: circuitpython_typing.AudioSample
documentation:
Classes that implement the audiosample protocol.
You can play these back with `audioio.AudioOut`, `audiobusio.I2SOut` or `audiopwmio.PWMAudioOut`.

variables.name: circuitpython_typing.FrameBuffer
documentation: Classes that implement the framebuffer protocol.

variables.name: circuitpython_typing.Alarm
documentation:
Classes that implement alarms for sleeping and asynchronous notification.
You can use these alarms to wake up from light or deep sleep.

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.ByteStream

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.ByteStream.read
documentation:
Read `count` bytes from the stream.
If `count` bytes are not immediately available,
or if the parameter is not specified in the call,
the outcome is implementation-dependent.

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.ByteStream.write
documentation: Write the bytes in `buf` to the stream.

Protocol for basic I/O operations on a byte stream.
Classes which implement this protocol include
* `io.BytesIO`
* `io.FileIO` (for a file open in binary mode)
* `busio.UART`
* `usb_cdc.Serial`

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.BlockDevice

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.BlockDevice.readblocks
documentation:
Read aligned, multiples of blocks. Starting at
the block given by the index `block_num`, read blocks
from the device into `buf` (an array of bytes). The number
of blocks to read is given by the length of `buf`,
which will be a multiple of the block size.

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.BlockDevice.writeblocks
documentation:
Write aligned, multiples of blocks, and require that
the blocks that are written to be first erased (if necessary)
by this method. Starting at the block given by the index
`block_num`, write blocks from `buf` (an array of bytes) to the
device. The number of blocks to write is given by the length
of `buf`, which will be a multiple of the block size.

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.BlockDevice.ioctl
documentation:
Control the block device and query its parameters. The operation to
perform is given by `operation` which is one of the following integers:

- 1 - initialise the device (`arg` is unused)
- 2 - shutdown the device (`arg` is unused)
- 3 - sync the device (`arg` is unused)
- 4 - get a count of the number of blocks, should return an integer (`arg` is unused)
- 5 - get the number of bytes in a block, should return an integer,
  : or `None` in which case the default value of 512 is used (`arg` is unused)
- 6 - erase a block, arg is the block number to erase

As a minimum `ioctl(4, ...)` must be intercepted; for littlefs `ioctl(6, ...)`
must also be intercepted. The need for others is hardware dependent.

Prior to any call to `writeblocks(block, ...)` littlefs issues `ioctl(6, block)`.
This enables a device driver to erase the block prior to a write if the hardware
requires it. Alternatively a driver might intercept `ioctl(6, block)` and return 0
(success). In this case the driver assumes responsibility for detecting the need
for erasure.

Unless otherwise stated `ioctl(operation, arg)` can return `None`. Consequently an
implementation can ignore unused values of `operation`. Where `operation` is
intercepted, the return value for operations 4 and 5 are as detailed above. Other
operations should return 0 on success and non-zero for failure, with the value returned
being an `OSError` errno code.

Protocol for block device objects to enable a device to support
CircuitPython filesystems. Classes which implement this protocol
include `storage.VfsFat`.

modules.name: circuitpython_typing.socket
documentation:
`circuitpython_typing.socket`
================================================================================

Type annotation definitions for sockets. Used for `adafruit_requests` and similar libraries.

* Author(s): Kevin Conley
SPDX-FileCopyrightText: Copyright (c) 2022 Kevin Conley
SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.CommonSocketType

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.CommonSocketType.send
documentation:
Send data to the socket. The meaning of the optional flags kwarg is
implementation-specific.

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.CommonSocketType.settimeout
documentation: Set a timeout on blocking socket operations.

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.CommonSocketType.close
documentation: Close the socket.
Describes the common structure every socket type must have.

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.CommonCircuitPythonSocketType

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.CommonCircuitPythonSocketType.connect
documentation:
Connect to a remote socket at the provided (host, port) address. The conntype
kwarg optionally may indicate SSL or not, depending on the underlying interface.
Describes the common structure every CircuitPython socket type must have.

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.LegacyCircuitPythonSocketType

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.LegacyCircuitPythonSocketType.recv
documentation:
Receive data from the socket. The return value is a bytes object representing
the data received. The maximum amount of data to be received at once is specified
by bufsize.
Describes the structure a legacy CircuitPython socket type must have.

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.SupportsRecvWithFlags

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.SupportsRecvWithFlags.recv
documentation:
Receive data from the socket. The return value is a bytes object representing
the data received. The maximum amount of data to be received at once is specified
by bufsize. The meaning of the optional flags kwarg is implementation-specific.
Describes a type that posseses a socket recv() method supporting the flags kwarg.

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.SupportsRecvInto

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.SupportsRecvInto.recv_into
documentation:
Receive up to nbytes bytes from the socket, storing the data into the provided
buffer. If nbytes is not specified (or 0), receive up to the size available in the
given buffer. The meaning of the optional flags kwarg is implementation-specific.
Returns the number of bytes received.
Describes a type that possesses a socket recv_into() method.

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.CircuitPythonSocketType
documentation: Describes the structure every modern CircuitPython socket type must have.

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.StandardPythonSocketType

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.StandardPythonSocketType.connect
documentation: Connect to a remote socket at the provided address.
Describes the structure every standard Python socket type must have.

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.InterfaceType

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.socket.InterfaceType.TLS_MODE
documentation: Constant representing that a socket's connection mode is TLS.
Describes the structure every interface type must have.

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.socket._FakeSSLSocket
documentation: Describes the structure every fake SSL socket type must have.
pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods

classes.name: circuitpython_typing.socket._FakeSSLContext

methods.name: circuitpython_typing.socket._FakeSSLContext.wrap_socket
documentation: Wrap socket and return a new one that uses the methods from the original.
Describes the structure every fake SSL context type must have.
